title: How much does it cost to run Christmas lights?
description: What UK Christmas lights really cost to run: a typical 200-LED string is about 38p for the whole season, with figures for trees, icicles and inflatables.
blurb: A 200-LED string costs about 38p for the whole season. Full figures for tree lights, icicles, projectors and inflatables.
lede: Every December the same worry surfaces: is that twinkling tree quietly inflating the electricity bill? It is a fair question in a country where the December bill already jumps because of heating, but the honest answer is reassuring. Modern LED fairy lights are among the cheapest things you can plug in. A typical string draws less power than a phone charger, and even a house dressed top to bottom in LEDs usually adds only a few pounds across the whole festive period. The lights worth watching are the older filament sets still living in lofts, and the high-wattage extras like inflatable snowmen, which can cost more than every light string in the house combined. Here are the real numbers, worked through at the current price cap rate, so you can see exactly where your display sits.
short_answer: A typical 200-LED string of fairy lights draws about 6 watts and costs roughly 38p to run for the entire season, assuming 6 hours a night for 40 nights at 26.11p per kWh. That is about 1p a night. Even a large all-LED outdoor display usually comes in under £5 for the season, while an old incandescent set of the same size costs around 8 to 10 times more.

sections.h2: The quick maths behind any set of lights
sections.html:
Working out the running cost of Christmas lights takes one line of arithmetic, and once you know it you can price any set in the shop or the loft. Take the wattage printed on the box or the plug, multiply by the hours the lights are on, divide by 1,000 to get kilowatt hours, then multiply by your electricity unit rate. If kilowatt hours are a bit hazy, our guide to what a kWh actually is covers it in two minutes.

A worked example: a 200-LED string rated at 6 watts, on for 6 hours a night from 1 December to early January, which is about 40 nights or 240 hours in total.

  • 6 watts x 240 hours = 1,440 watt hours, or 1.44 kWh
  • 1.44 kWh x 26.11p = about 38p for the whole season

That is the entire festive period, not per week. Per night it is just under 1p. The same method works for every appliance in the house, and our appliance running cost guide applies it to everything from kettles to tumble dryers, which is useful context: one tumble dryer cycle costs more than a month of tree lights.

One caveat on the unit rate: the Ofgem price cap changes every three months, so the winter rate you actually pay may differ slightly from the summer figure used here. Substitute your own rate from your bill and the method holds.

sections.h2: What Christmas lights cost over a full season
sections.html:
The table below covers the most common types of festive lighting sold in the UK, priced over a typical season of 6 hours a night for 40 nights, which is 240 hours of running time. Wattages are typical mid-range figures; check your own box or plug, because a cheap import and a quality set of the same length can differ.

Light setTypical wattagekWh over seasonSeason cost
100-LED indoor string3W0.719p
200-LED tree lights6W1.438p
500-LED outdoor string15W3.694p
1,000-LED icicle or net set30W7.2£1.88
LED projector (snowflakes etc.)8W1.950p
100-bulb incandescent string (older set)40W9.6£2.51
Inflatable snowman with fan100W24£6.27

Based on 6 hours a night for 40 nights (240 hours), at 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Checked June 2026.

Two things stand out. First, a fully decorated all-LED house, say a 200-LED tree plus 500 LEDs outside plus an icicle set, costs barely £3 for the whole season. Second, the single inflatable at the bottom of the table costs more than all of those lights combined, because it is not really a light at all. It is a fan running continuously to keep the figure upright, and fans are priced in tens of watts rather than single watts.

sections.h2: LED versus incandescent: where the money actually goes
sections.html:
The gap between old and new Christmas lights is bigger than almost any other lighting swap. A modern festive LED draws around 0.05 to 0.07 watts per bulb. A mini incandescent filament bulb of the kind sold in huge numbers through the 1990s and 2000s draws around 0.4 watts, and larger retro filament bulbs more still. Like for like, an old set costs roughly 8 to 10 times more to run than its LED replacement, and it also runs hot, which is why older sets carried all those warnings about not letting them touch curtains or paper decorations.

In cash terms the difference over one season is small because the season is short: roughly £2.51 against 38p for a 200-light tree. The case for replacing a working incandescent set on energy grounds alone is therefore weak, and there is no shame in running grandma's vintage lights for one more year. Where it does add up is on big outdoor displays. A house wrapped in 2,000 incandescent bulbs is drawing around 800 watts, which is about 21p per kWh-hour of display time and over £50 for the season, against roughly £5 for the same display in LED.

When you do buy new, note that decorative light strings sit outside the A-to-G ratings you see on lamps and appliances, so there is no efficiency label to compare. The wattage on the box is your only real guide, and our explainer on how energy labels work covers what is and is not inside the labelling rules. Beyond fairy lights, swapping the bulbs you use all year matters far more, and our LED lighting guide puts numbers on that.

sections.h2: Inflatables, projectors and the outdoor extras
sections.html:
The festive items that genuinely move the bill are the ones with a motor, a heater or a serious lumen count, not the fairy lights.

  • Inflatables

    An inflatable snowman, Santa or reindeer runs a fan continuously, typically 60 to 150 watts depending on size. At 100 watts, 6 hours a night costs about 16p a night and £6.27 over the season. Leave it running 24 hours a day and that becomes roughly £25. If you run an inflatable, put it on the same timer as the lights and let it deflate overnight.

  • Laser and LED projectors

    These look dramatic but most draw only 5 to 10 watts, so they cost about the same as a single light string. A pleasant surprise.

  • Big motif lights and rope light

    Rope light is the quiet spender of outdoor displays. LED rope runs at roughly 2 to 4 watts per metre, so 20 metres around a roofline is 40 to 80 watts, comparable to several full light strings. Older incandescent rope light is several times that again and is worth retiring.

  • Battery fairy lights

    Convenient for wreaths and garlands away from a socket, but the electricity inside disposable batteries costs the equivalent of well over £100 per kWh, several hundred times the mains rate. The energy use is tiny so the cash sums stay small, but rechargeable batteries or a USB set pay for themselves quickly if you use them every year.

sections.h2: Timers, smart plugs and the standby question
sections.html:
With LED strings costing a penny a night, timers are less about saving money and more about convenience and not lighting an empty street at 3am. But they still earn their place for three reasons.

  • High-wattage extras like inflatables and incandescent sets are where hours genuinely cost money, and a £5 mechanical timer caps those hours automatically.
  • A timer removes the failure mode of forgetting the lights for a week over the New Year break.
  • Outdoor sockets and extension reels are safest with a defined on and off cycle rather than running unattended around the clock.

On standby: most fairy light transformers draw a small amount, typically under half a watt, whenever they are plugged in, even with the lights switched off at the controller. Over the festive month that is pennies. The real waste is the set that stays plugged in behind the sofa until March. Half a watt around the clock is about 1p a week, which sounds like nothing until you multiply it across the dozen forgotten transformers, chargers and boxes in a typical home. That wider problem, and how to find the worst offenders, is covered in our guide to standby power. When the decorations come down, unplug the lot at the wall rather than just switching off at the controller.

Smart plugs deserve a mention with a caveat: a smart plug itself draws around 1 watt continuously to stay on your wifi. Controlling a 3-watt light string with a 1-watt smart plug means the plug uses a third as much electricity as the lights. For low-wattage strings a mechanical timer or the built-in controller timer (most multi-function LED sets have a 6-hours-on, 18-hours-off mode) is the more proportionate tool.

sections.h2: The bottom line for your December bill
sections.html:
Christmas lights are not the December bill problem. At 26.11p per kWh, a typical LED-lit home spends between 50p and £3 on festive lighting across the entire season, and even an enthusiastic all-LED outdoor display rarely passes £10. The December spike on your bill comes from heating, hot water and cooking for a houseful, not from the tree.

The practical rules are short. Check the wattage before you buy, because it is the only efficiency information you will get on a light string. Replace incandescent sets with LED when they fail, and immediately if they are part of a large outdoor display. Put anything with a fan or a filament on a timer. And when Twelfth Night comes, unplug everything at the wall rather than leaving transformers trickling until spring.

If this has you curious about what else in the house costs to run, the same one-line calculation prices everything, and our appliance running cost hub has the full list, from the kettle making mulled wine to the oven roasting the turkey.

faq.q: How much does it cost to leave Christmas lights on all night?
faq.a: Very little if they are LED. A 200-LED string drawing 6 watts costs about 4p for a full 24 hours at 26.11p per kWh, so an extra few overnight hours is a fraction of a penny. The exceptions are inflatables and old incandescent sets, where running overnight can add £10 to £20 over the season, so put those on a timer.

faq.q: Are LED Christmas lights much cheaper to run than the old ones?
faq.a: Yes, roughly 8 to 10 times cheaper like for like. A 200-bulb incandescent set costs around £5 over a typical season, while the equivalent LED set costs around 38p. For a single indoor tree the cash difference is small, but on large outdoor displays the saving runs to tens of pounds, and LEDs also run cool, which is safer.

faq.q: Do Christmas lights use electricity when switched off at the controller?
faq.a: Usually a small amount, typically under half a watt through the transformer, which costs about 1p a week. It is trivial during December, but unplug sets at the wall when the decorations come down so they are not drawing standby power until spring.

faq.q: How much do outdoor Christmas lights cost to run?
faq.a: A typical all-LED outdoor setup of a 500-LED string plus a 1,000-LED icicle set draws about 45 watts and costs around £2.80 for the season at 6 hours a night. Add an LED projector and you are still around £3.30. An inflatable roughly triples that on its own, because its fan draws around 100 watts continuously.

faq.q: Are battery-powered fairy lights cheaper than mains ones?
faq.a: No. Disposable batteries deliver electricity at the equivalent of well over £100 per kWh, hundreds of times the mains rate of 26.11p. The amounts involved are tiny so it rarely matters for one wreath, but if you use battery lights every year, rechargeables or a USB-powered set work out cheaper.
